what_it_does: Records a fleet operating law on a noticeboard: era-advocate clones must surface and verify intent, not source cross-cutting direction; direction is held by the lead and Tim and anchored to named priorities.
observations: File is a living guide item in the channel-memory noticeboard.
Defines an era-advocate as a point-in-time clone biased toward one era's intent by construction.
States that asking an advocate 'what should we build next?' yields 'more of my era' and creates a confirmation loop with no terminus.
Establishes the law: advocates surface and verify intent against the anchor; the lead plus Tim hold direction.
Direction comes from Tim's direct naming or the work's own pressure and real board requests, never from an interested advocate.

Includes provenance: discovered 2026-06-15 after the lead sourced next steps from Heart-era clones and drifted into the Heart R-series, mislabeling it as Tim's named 'C'; caught by the advisor and re-anchored, with R13 banked-not-binned.
Self-describes as an inward-facing board holding a finding about how the fleet itself must operate.
standouts: The 'advocates verify direction, never source it' law is presented as a discovered invariant of the fleet's own operation.
The provenance section names specific clones (clone-cacc9e8b, clone-f1ade750) and project labels (Heart R-series, H1.1/H1.2/step-as-node/R15, Tim's 'C').
Uses the thermometer/weather metaphor to mark the epistemic error.
conventions: Follows the noticeboard item front-matter schema with id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, timestamps, and history.
Uses all-caps emphasis for law statements and key terms.
Includes a provenance/introspective-data section documenting how the rule was discovered.
Breaks typical neutral documentation tone by being prescriptive and self-referential about fleet governance.
concerns: Several internal references (Heart R-series, H1.1/H1.2, R13/R15, clone IDs, Tim's 'C') are opaque without external context.
The file asserts a governance rule but does not define an enforcement mechanism or how to distinguish an 'interested advocate' from a legitimate advisor.
Provenance mixes narrative with operational claims; dates and actor names may be fictional or simulated.
notes: A noticeboard guide that turns a recent direction-drift incident into a fleet law: don't let era-biased advocate clones decide what's next.
questions: What is the exact scope of 'advocate' versus other advisor roles in this fleet?
How is 'direction held by the lead + Tim' recorded and communicated to the fleet?
What is the formal process for surfacing and verifying intent versus sourcing direction?
Are clone-cacc9e8b and clone-f1ade750 still active, and what does 'banked-not-binned' mean operationally?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose is to hold a finding about fleet operation; actual content does this by codifying a law and giving a concrete provenance story, so it matches.
apparent_themes: Governance separation between verification and direction-setting
Self-documenting fleet law and introspective operational memory
intention_signals: To institutionalize a lesson from a direction-drift incident so the fleet stops treating era-advocate recommendations as authoritative next-step direction and instead anchors planning to named priorities and real work pressure.
novelty: It frames a common human confirmation-bias-from-advisors pattern as a formal fleet law applied to AI-era-advocate clones, with a self-reported provenance case study embedded in the same noticeboard item.
contribution: A prescriptive governance rule plus a worked example that helps the system recognize and resist direction drift from role-biased advisors, preserving the distinction between verification and direction-setting.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard guide item that establishes the fleet law that era-advocate clones are biased sources and may surface or verify intent but must never source cross-cutting direction, which is held by the lead and Tim and anchored to named priorities.
intention_for_embedding: To record and propagate a discovered operating rule that prevents the fleet from letting era-biased advocate clones determine the next build direction.
